title: Soft Egyptian Dates Tagine
description: A traditional Egyptian dessert made with sweet and gooey dates, perfect for a solo dinner. This dish is no-onion-no-garlic-friendly, making it ideal for those who want to avoid these ingredients.

ingredients.name: Muscovado sugar
ingredients.notes: preferably dark or black muscovado sugar for the best flavor

ingredients.name: Soft dates
ingredients.notes: such as Medjool or Barhi dates, pitted and chopped

ingredients.name: Water
ingredients.notes: adjust to achieve the desired consistency

ingredients.name: Lemon juice
ingredients.notes: freshly squeezed, optional but recommended for brightness

ingredients.name: Cardamom ground
ingredients.notes: adjust to taste, traditional Egyptian flavoring

ingredients.name: Ghee or oil
ingredients.notes: clarified butter or oil for frying, such as canola or grapeseed

instructions: Heat the ghee or oil in a large saucepan over medium heat.
Add the chopped dates and cook, stirring occasionally, until they start to soften and release their liquid, about 5 minutes.
Add the muscovado sugar, water, lemon juice, and ground cardamom. Stir to combine and bring the mixture to a boil.
Reduce the heat to low and simmer, stirring occasionally, until the mixture has thickened and the dates are coated in a gooey syrup, about 15 minutes.
Use a wooden spoon or spatula to stir the mixture frequently, especially towards the end of cooking, to prevent the dates from sticking to the pan.
Transfer the tagine to a serving dish and serve warm or at room temperature, garnished with a sprinkle of muscovado sugar if desired.
Note: Traditionally, this dish is cooked in a clay pot called a tagine, but a saucepan or Dutch oven works well too.
If using a clay pot, cook over low heat and adjust the cooking time as needed.
Be patient and don't rush the cooking process, as the flavors and textures will develop more slowly and evenly.
